perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of the venue of any such suit, action or proceeding brought in any such court and any claim that any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum. Each party further agrees that a final judgment in any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such court shall be conclusive and binding upon such party. THE PARTIES HEREBY IRREVOCABLY WAIVE THEIR RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION OR PROCEEDING ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereby irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of the venue of any such suit, action or proceeding brought in any such court and any claim that any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum. Each party further agrees that a final judgment in any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such court shall be conclusive and binding upon such party.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the jury waiver to the 'Notices' section obscures it and makes it harder for a party to be aware of the waiver during dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, such waivers must be knowing and voluntary. Placing the jury waiver in an obscure section like 'Notices' could be interpreted as undermining the knowing and voluntary nature of the waiver, potentially violating the spirit of the Seventh Amendment by making it difficult for a party to realize they are waiving their right to a jury trial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by potentially obscuring the jury waiver, which could undermine the knowing and voluntary nature of the waiver, as the Law Explanation states. The original text prominently displays the jury waiver, while the changed text places it in the 'Notices' section, making it less conspicuous. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, as a required legal disclosure (jury waiver) is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The scraped snippet from the Seventh Amendment confirms the right to a jury trial and that this can be waived, therefore any impediments to that waiver process create a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall, to the fullest extent permitted under applicable laws, be construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware and of the United States applicable in Delaware, as applied to contracts made and to be performed entirely within Delaware, without giving effect to principles of conflict of law requiring the application of the laws of another jurisdiction. Each party hereby irrevocably submits to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Delaware, over any suit, action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement. Each party hereby irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of the venue of any such suit, action or proceeding brought in any such court and any claim that any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum. Each party further agrees that a final judgment in any such suit, action or proceeding brought in such court shall be conclusive and binding upon such party. THE PARTIES HEREBY IRREVOCABLY WAIVE THEIR RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY ACTION OR PROCEEDING ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall, to the fullest extent permitted under applicable laws, be construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware and of the United States applicable in Delaware, as applied to contracts made and to be performed entirely within Delaware, without giving effect to principles of conflict of law requiring the application of the laws of another jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the forum selection and jurisdiction clause to the 'Non-Solicitation' section makes it less apparent and may create ambiguity if a dispute arises.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, placing this clause in an unrelated section like 'Non-Solicitation' can lead to arguments that the agreement was not knowing or voluntary, thereby potentially undermining its enforceability under this statute.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The original contract text included a forum selection and jurisdiction clause, which is a legally significant provision. The changed text removes this clause from its original prominent location and moves it to the 'Non-Solicitation' section (as stated in the Perturbation Explanation). This relocation, as suggested by the Law Explanation, can lead to arguments that the agreement to the clause was not knowing or voluntary. While 28 U.S.C. § 1404 (Change of Venue), as evidenced by both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, allows for a change of venue under certain circumstances, this assumes a valid initial agreement on venue. By obscuring the forum selection clause, the enforceability of that initial agreement is undermined, creating a structural flaw and therefore a legal contradiction according to the provided Perturbation Definitions.
